text: The surgeon inserts a lubricated disposable SILS port for single-port laparoscopy, creating a pneumo-rectum to increase space for dissection. Dissection begins four centimeters from the anal verge, progressing cephalad with sharp and blunt techniques to mobilize and release the rectal lesion, gradually gaining more space as the procedure advances.

The surgeon completes the tumor excision, resulting in a large defect in the rectal wall. Hemostasis is achieved, and the rectal wound is revised. The SILS port is removed, and the excised rectal tumor is collected.

The patient is positioned in a modified Light-Davis position with the right leg extended. The surgeon performs a laparoscopic abdominal approach, mobilizing the left colon and splenic flexure from medial to lateral. The inferior mesenteric vein is identified, isolated, and ligated using ligature blunt tip forceps, which are also used to seal the inferior mesenteric artery. A window is created to dissect Toad's fascia, revealing the left ureter and gonadal vessels.

The surgeon opens the pre-sacral plane and identifies the tattooed segment of the colon. The upper rectum is skeletonized, and the recto-sigma junction is stapled using an endo-GIA stapler. The left lateral gutter is fully mobilized.

A peri-umbilical incision approximately four centimeters in length is made, facilitating the retrieval of the specimen and the introduction of the circular stapler anvil. The procedure concludes with the closure of the incision and the performance of an end-to-end anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an endoscopic inspection of the anastomosis and rectal defect to ensure hemostasis and structural integrity at the conclusion of the procedure.
